The Wise Woman
Review:
WORST BOOK I HAVE EVER READ SPURRED ME TO WRITE MY 1ST REVIEW:
wow. let me say first that the ending of this book is like a bucket of ice water on a freezing day - painful and out of nowhere. FYI: This book reads more like a BAD pulp novel than anything and makes the Wideacre sex scenes seem virginal - there is violent sex, a threesome, & rape.

I've read nearly everything by PG and while she's awful when it comes to historical accuracy, her novels are undoubtedly entertaining. This book isn't even as darkly entertaining as her Wideacre series, its just awful in its execution. I read this book only because the bad reviews for that series made me consider that this might be in the "so bad its good" category along with it.

The novel suffers from a disjointed narrative that seems to span years yet only covers 10 months in the life of a runaway nun named Alys, fleeing the destruction of her abbey under the reign of Henry VIII. She flees to her foster mother but desires the relative luxury of the abbey to peasant life. This desire for luxury coupled with a desire for security drives her throughout the novel to commit greater and greater transgressions from her vows. However, she changes course so often as to give you whiplash, going from trying to maintain her vows to lusting after clothes, food, wine, and sex and back again. I suppose PG meant this to feel more like an internal struggle of faith v. worldliness but it was handled so badly that it just feels heavy handed. Predictably, Alys' past catches up to her and she must deal with seeing a mentor suffer under the king's heresy laws. The books ends so abruptly after this confrontation, and with what starts off sounding like a dream (in italics) ends before you realize it.

Without doubt the worst of PG's books, no finesse and no inspiration.

Review:
If this had been a hardcopy book and not a kindle edition, I'd have thrown it at the wall in disgust. "what lies outside the gates" isn't nearly the revelation it could have been. It was incredibly boring and I kept waiting for something more. What I got instead was a ridiculous Russian nesting doll-esque conspiracy storylines that seemed more ridiculous with every 'twist'

Roth's big twist ending is certainly against the grain of other dystopian-with-a-feminine-heroine types of fiction but it wasn't satisfying and left me feeling duped. I had to force myself to continue after that point.

annoyed I read beyond the first book. my only excuse is that i was bored and wanted a brainless read. certainly got that....
